county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1915 from parts of Madison and Fremont counties. Eastern Idaho county on the west side of the Teton Range. Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Teton County Recorder in Driggs has deed records from 1915. Teton County sits in the Teton Valley on the west side of the Teton Range, historically known as Pierre's Hole — the famous 1832 fur trade rendezvous occurred here. LDS ward records are primary genealogical sources for most Teton County families, with wards in Driggs, Victor, and Tetonia organized in the 1890s-1900s; records are indexed on FamilySearch. The Teton Valley Historical Museum in Driggs has pioneer family files and photographs. Check Madison and Fremont counties for pre-1915 records. The Teton Dam collapse of 1976 affected the upper end of the valley; flood relief records document impacted families. Alta and the Teton Range attracted mountain guides and dude ranch operators. The Teton Valley News newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS. Potato and grain farming shaped the valley's agricultural economy.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1915 to present. County Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1915. County Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1947.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: State registration from 1911; county-filed records may predate 1911. Certified copies from Idaho Bureau of Vital Records. Some counties have earlier records filed with the county recorder.
Civil and criminal case files, probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, and name changes from 1915.
Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, and probate. Browse-only image collections from Teton County Recorder's office.
Federal population schedules for Teton County. Idaho Territory enumerated from 1870. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide additional family details.

source_profiles.repository: Idaho State Historical Society / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers from Teton County communities. Idaho State Historical Society Newspaper Archives and Chronicling America have digitized Idaho papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1907 from Cassia County. South-central Idaho's regional center. Magic Valley agricultural hub.
county_metadata.research_tips: Twin Falls County Recorder in Twin Falls has deed records from 1907. Twin Falls was a planned city, established in 1904 by I.B. Perrine who envisioned irrigating the Snake River Plain using Milner Dam — the Twin Falls Land and Water Company sold tracts to settlers, and company records at ISHS document original purchasers with detailed biographical information. The Magic Valley irrigation boom attracted families from across the Midwest. The Twin Falls County Historical Society museum has pioneer family files and irrigation-era records. Evel Knievel's attempted Snake River Canyon jump (1974) brought national attention. Japanese-American farmers were present before WWII internment at Minidoka. Check Cassia County for pre-1907 records. The College of Southern Idaho (1965) in Twin Falls holds regional records. The Twin Falls Times-News newspaper archives extend to 1904.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from parts of Boise and Idaho counties. Central Idaho mountain county. Includes McCall and the Payette Lake resort area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Valley County Recorder in Cascade has deed records from 1917. Valley County encompasses the Long Valley and the Payette Lake area around McCall. The timber industry dominated — Boise Payette Lumber Company, Brown Tie and Lumber Company, and other operations generated employment records at the University of Idaho Library and ISHS. McCall was a lumber town that later became a resort community; the Central Idaho Historical Museum in McCall has logging-era family files. Check Boise and Idaho counties for pre-1917 records. Cascade was partially relocated when Cascade Dam created Cascade Reservoir (1948); Bureau of Reclamation records document affected property owners. Yellow Pine and Stibnite were remote mining camps — antimony mining during WWII generated strategic mineral records at NARA. Donnelly, Lake Fork, and other small communities have distinct settlement histories tied to logging and ranching. The Star-News newspaper in McCall documents the local community.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1879 from parts of Boise and Ada counties. Western Idaho county on the Oregon border. Agricultural and ranching community.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County Recorder in Weiser holds deed records from 1879. Weiser is at the confluence of the Weiser River and Snake River on the Oregon border. The Oregon Short Line railroad brought settlers in the 1880s. Weiser is famous for the National Old-Time Fiddlers' Contest (since 1953), which has drawn musicians from across the country. The Washington County Historical Society museum has pioneer family files and photographs. The Indian Valley and the Weiser River corridor attracted ranchers and farmers; livestock brand records at ISHS identify early stockmen. Gold mining at various locations in the county generated mining district records at ISHS and BLM. Cambridge, Midvale, and Indian Valley communities have distinct settlement histories. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries. The Weiser Signal American newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS. Some Chinese miners worked along the Weiser River in the 1860s-1870s; tax and census records survive at ISHS.
